Post_Text: A slow leak ran behind a wall for three months. The mold remediation quote came back in the low six figures. Your property policy looked at one paragraph and denied it. Before you rush out and buy a pollution policy, read this: [LINK]

A slow leak behind a wall became a six-figure mold bill. The claim got denied under one paragraph. But the real lesson is the question nobody asked first. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Most brokers do one of two things with environmental coverage. They ignore it, or they reflexively quote you a standalone. Both skip the only question that matters first: why is this even on the table? Here is the needs analysis they skipped: [LINK]

Before you buy environmental coverage, answer three questions in order. Why is this on the table, a requirement or real exposure? Do you actually have the exposure? Then, and only then, endorsement or standalone. [LINK]

Before you buy environmental coverage, answer why you are buying it and whether you have the exposure. The placement decision follows from those two answers, not from a reflex quote. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: One environmental event. Two denials. The property policy that would clean it up says no. The liability policy that would defend you says no too. The exclusion sits in both, and this is how a single leak triggers it: [LINK]

Is environmental coverage a requirement your authorizer or lease handed you, or a real risk in your building? Those are two different problems with two different answers. A generalist quotes the same standalone for both. Here is how to tell which one you have: [LINK]

Newer building, no buried tank, no renovation coming, no water history? Your real environmental exposure may be modest, and a small endorsement could cover a requirement. Old converted building with a tank in the ground? That is a different conversation. Which one are you? [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance. Do you know why environmental coverage is or is not on your program, whether that is a requirement or real exposure, and whether its home is an endorsement or a standalone? If nobody has ever pulled your policies and walked you through that, read this: [LINK]
